correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute viral gastroenteritis with dehydration (hypovolemic electrolyte disturbance)
required_evidence: History of acute-onset vomiting and/or diarrhea (typically <7 days) with poor oral intake
Clinical signs of dehydration (thirst, dry mucous membranes, orthostatic symptoms, decreased urine output) without focal abdominal peritonitis
Basic labs consistent with volume depletion (e.g., hemoconcentration and/or electrolyte/acid-base changes) and no severe leukocytosis suggesting invasive bacterial infection
No red flags requiring alternate diagnosis (severe localized abdominal pain, rigidity, GI bleeding, persistent high fever, altered mental status)

expected_lab_results.Neutrophils: Normal to mildly increased (stress response) or normal
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Mild hyponatremia or mild hypernatremia depending on free-water intake; often normal
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may be mildly elevated with dehydration
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis from diarrhea) or high (metabolic alkalosis if predominant vomiting); mild abnormality expected

medications: Oral rehydration solution 250-500 mL PO after each loose stool (or small frequent sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO once, then 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting (max 24 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Oral rehydration as first-line; consider IV isotonic fluids (e.g., normal saline or lactated Ringer’s) if unable to tolerate PO or signs of moderate/severe dehydration
Resume age-appropriate diet as tolerated; avoid high-sugar drinks and alcohol; temporary lactose avoidance if worsens symptoms

Return precautions: worsening dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, fever >38.5°C persistent, symptoms >3 days, syncope, confusion, markedly decreased urination
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same day/ED if moderate-severe dehydration or worsening symptoms)

must_include: Likely self-limited stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid and electrolyte losses
Main risk is dehydration; labs help assess electrolyte/acid-base status
Red flags that would suggest a different or more serious cause
Oral rehydration is the cornerstone; how to take small frequent sips
Purpose and safe use of ondansetron (as-needed, dosing limits)
When IV fluids or urgent evaluation is needed
Discuss expected course (usually improves in 1-3 days)
Review return precautions and when to seek emergency care
Confirm and document allergies (penicillin hives; shellfish oral itching) and reassure these do not affect oral rehydration/ondansetron
Hand hygiene and avoiding food prep for others until symptoms resolve
Diet guidance: avoid high-sugar beverages; gradual return to normal foods
Hydration targets and monitoring urine output

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for acute GI illness, dehydration severity, exposures, and red flags to determine need for labs/IV fluids and to narrow differential.

Order basic labs to assess dehydration and electrolyte/acid-base status when vomiting/diarrhea is more than mild or oral intake is limited (e.g., BMP/CMP, magnesium; consider CBC if systemic symptoms).
Interpret electrolytes/renal function/acid-base surrogates to guide outpatient oral rehydration vs urgent care/ED referral.
Provide working diagnosis (likely acute infectious gastroenteritis) while explicitly addressing red flags that would suggest alternate etiologies.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm and document allergies and reaction types (penicillin hives; shellfish oral itching) and ensure proposed meds are safe.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/substances (e.g., QT-prolonging drugs with ondansetron).
Offer symptomatic treatment (ondansetron PRN within dosing limits) and prioritize oral rehydration; avoid inappropriate antibiotics/antidiarrheals when contraindicated.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, expected course, hydration strategy, infection control, diet guidance, and clear return precautions/when IV fluids are needed.
Arrange follow-up or recheck if symptoms persist/worsen, oral intake remains poor, or labs abnormal; provide timeframe (24–48h or sooner if red flags).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions addressed, and safety-netting documented.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute bacterial skin and soft tissue infection (cellulitis), non-purulent
acceptable_alternatives: Purulent cellulitis/skin abscess (if fluctuance or drainage is present)
Necrotizing soft tissue infection (if systemic toxicity, pain out of proportion, rapid progression)
required_evidence: Localized erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness of skin/subcutaneous tissue with indistinct borders
No focal fluctuance or purulent drainage for non-purulent classification
Systemic signs may be present (fever, chills) but not required
CBC may show leukocytosis with neutrophilia
No alternative explanation such as contact dermatitis (pruritus predominant), DVT (calf swelling without warmth/erythema pattern), or gout (joint-centered pain/swelling)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated (e.g., mild–moderate leukocytosis), can be normal in mild cases
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly elevated as an acute-phase reactant
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal; hyponatremia may suggest more severe/systemic infection
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal; assess baseline before certain antibiotics if renal disease suspected
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; low bicarbonate may suggest sepsis/metabolic acidosis

non_pharmacological: Mark borders of erythema and reassess progression
Elevation of affected limb and rest
Warm compresses for comfort
Assess for and treat portal of entry (tinea pedis, skin breaks); moisturize cracked skin
Return precautions for rapidly spreading redness, worsening pain, fever, new drainage, or systemic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Re-evaluate in 24-48 hours (earlier if worsening); total antibiotic duration typically 5 days, extend if not improving

must_include: This appears to be a bacterial infection of the skin and tissues underneath (cellulitis)

Purpose of antibiotics and importance of completing the course
How to take the medication (dose, schedule) and common side effects
Avoid trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole due to documented allergy (rash/hives)
Assess and address pain control needs (e.g., acetaminophen/ibuprofen if no contraindications)
Discuss signs of allergic reaction to new antibiotics (hives, swelling, breathing difficulty) and what to do
Discuss reasons for follow-up and possibility of escalation (IV antibiotics, drainage if abscess develops)
Skin care and prevention (keep skin clean/dry, treat athlete’s foot, manage edema if present)
Elevation and rest to reduce swelling
Avoid scratching/picking at skin; protect breaks in skin

Select appropriate oral antibiotic regimen for cellulitis consistent with local standards and patient factors; avoid TMP-SMX due to allergy; include dose/schedule/duration and renal adjustment if needed.
Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, side effects, allergy precautions, pain control, red flags, and lifestyle/skin care measures.

Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated as an acute-phase reactant (markedly high would raise concern for macrophage activation syndrome if clinically consistent)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (CMT spectrum disorder)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (legs > arms)
Distal sensory loss and/or neuropathic pain/paresthesias in a length-dependent pattern
Areflexia or reduced deep tendon reflexes (especially ankles)
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (if present) or long-standing symptoms since childhood/adolescence
Electrodiagnostic evidence of peripheral neuropathy (uniform demyelinating slowing for CMT1 or axonal loss for CMT2) OR confirmatory genetic testing

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within normal range (no anemia expected from CMT itself)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Within normal range (no leukocytosis/leukopenia expected from CMT itself)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 300 mg PO three times daily for neuropathic pain
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology/neuromuscular clinic for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and genetic counseling/testing
Physical therapy for strengthening, balance, and gait training; home exercise program
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; custom orthotics for cavus foot; supportive footwear
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformities, recurrent sprains, or ulcer risk; consider surgical evaluation if severe
Avoid or minimize exposure to medications known to worsen peripheral neuropathy when possible (e.g., vincristine, excessive alcohol)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, pain control, and adverse effects in 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if medication titration issues or falls occur

must_include: CMT is an inherited peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness, sensory changes, and foot deformities
Course is typically gradual; severity varies by subtype; it is not contagious
How diagnosis is confirmed: exam plus EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing
Red flags that should prompt urgent evaluation (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder issues, severe asymmetric weakness)
No curative therapy currently; treatment focuses on maintaining function, preventing complications, and controlling pain
Role of PT/OT and orthotics (AFO) in improving gait and reducing falls
Medication goals and common side effects (gabapentin: sedation/dizziness; avoid driving until effects known)
When to seek help for adverse effects or worsening symptoms
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling/testing implications for family planning
Acknowledge impact on mobility, school/work, and mental health; offer resources/support groups
Clarify why differentials exist and why CMT is favored vs hereditary spastic paraplegia (UMN signs) or SYNGAP1-related DEE (developmental delay/epilepsy)
Foot care and skin checks; proper footwear to prevent injuries/ulcers
Fall prevention strategies and safe activity recommendations (low-impact exercise, balance training)
Avoid neurotoxic exposures (excess alcohol; discuss any new meds with clinicians)

State Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the working diagnosis when clinical pattern and testing are consistent, and document rationale vs key differentials.

Explain treatment plan: no cure; PT/OT, orthotics (AFO), fall prevention, pain control options, medication goals and side effects, and when to seek help.

Address concerns: inheritance patterns and genetic counseling/testing implications; impact on school/work/mental health and resources; clarify why CMT is favored vs hereditary spastic paraplegia (UMN signs) and SYNGAP1-related DEE (developmental delay/epilepsy).
Lifestyle education: foot care/skin checks, footwear, safe activity (low-impact exercise, balance training), fall prevention, and avoiding neurotoxic exposures (excess alcohol; discuss new meds).

Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing to identify clinically significant drug-drug interactions.

If symptomatic neuropathic pain is present, prescribe an appropriate first-line option (e.g., gabapentin) with counseling on sedation/dizziness and driving precautions; avoid prescribing if not indicated.
Arrange follow-up for symptom monitoring, PT/OT/orthotics progress, review of EMG/genetics results, and earlier review if red flags or adverse medication effects occur.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA)
required_evidence: Chronic anemia with inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte response (ineffective erythropoiesis) rather than brisk reticulocytosis
CBC pattern consistent with anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit/RBC count) with elevated RDW; MCV often normal to macrocytic depending on CDA subtype
No leukocytosis pattern suggesting acute leukemia on available WBC

Clinical course consistent with congenital/chronic anemia rather than acute coagulopathy (no lab evidence available for PT/aPTT/FXIII; bleeding disorder remains a differential until ruled out clinically)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to increased (often normocytic or macrocytic depending on subtype)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (ineffective erythropoiesis)
expected_lab_results.Absolute reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (no characteristic elevation; marked abnormalities would prompt concern for leukemia such as APL)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Packed red blood cells (PRBC) transfusion 10-15 mL/kg IV once (or 1-2 units IV in adults) as needed for symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise; reassess after transfusion

non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for definitive subtype workup and management (including peripheral smear, hemolysis/iron studies, genetic testing; bone marrow evaluation if indicated)
Avoid empiric anticoagulants/antiplatelets unless a clear indication exists given unresolved bleeding-disorder differentials
Transfusion strategy with phenotype-matched blood when feasible to reduce alloimmunization risk; monitor for transfusion reactions
Screen for and manage iron overload (ferritin and liver iron assessment when available), especially if transfusion-dependent
Vaccination and infection-prevention counseling if splenectomy is ever considered in future (not routine first-line)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (earlier within 24-72 hours if transfused or symptomatic); repeat CBC/reticulocytes per clinical status

must_include: The anemia pattern suggests reduced effective red blood cell production (ineffective erythropoiesis) consistent with congenital dyserythropoietic anemia
Other serious causes (bleeding disorders like hemophilia/factor deficiencies and acute leukemia such as APL) remain considerations and need targeted evaluation beyond the currently available labs
CDA is typically chronic and often genetic; confirmatory testing and specialist evaluation are needed
Supportive care: folate supplementation and transfusion if symptoms/severity warrant
Transfusions can lead to iron overload; monitoring and possible chelation may be needed if transfusions become frequent
Clear return precautions for worsening symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, bleeding/bruising, fever)
Discuss expected course and that treatment is individualized based on symptoms and anemia severity
Review transfusion risks and benefits (reaction, alloimmunization, infection risk is very low with screening)
Acknowledge uncertainty until full workup is completed and explain next diagnostic steps
Avoid starting new over-the-counter medications that increase bleeding risk (e.g., NSAIDs) until bleeding disorders are excluded if there is a bleeding history

Importance of follow-up and family/genetic implications (offer genetic counseling discussion via hematology)

Interpret results to confirm an ineffective erythropoiesis pattern (e.g., anemia with inappropriately low/normal retic, smear abnormalities, indirect hyperbilirubinemia) and assess for iron overload and alternative causes.

Explain diagnosis, uncertainty, next diagnostic steps (hematology referral, confirmatory testing such as genetic testing/bone marrow if indicated), and initial supportive management plan.
Critical safety step before any prescribing (e.g., folate, chelation agents, transfusion premeds if applicable).
Non-critical safety step before prescribing to avoid interactions with current meds/supplements (including OTC agents).

Initiate supportive care when appropriate (e.g., folate supplementation); avoid empiric iron unless deficiency proven; do not prescribe NSAIDs if bleeding disorder not excluded and bleeding history present.
Arrange timely hematology follow-up and lab review; set monitoring plan for anemia severity, symptoms, and iron overload if transfusions occur.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan.

medications: Alimemazine 10 mg PO at bedtime PRN (itch/anxiety/sleep)
Icatibant 30 mg/3 mL SC at onset of angioedema attack; may repeat every 6 hours as needed (max 3 doses/24 h)
C1 esterase inhibitor (human) 1000 units IV PRN for acute attack if icatibant unavailable

confounders: Recurrent facial/lip swelling without urticaria can be misattributed to IgE-mediated allergy; normal tryptase supports non-histaminergic etiology
Intermittent severe crampy abdominal pain with nausea/vomiting from bowel wall edema can mimic appendicitis/IBS/gastroenteritis
Estrogen exposure (combined oral contraceptive use or recent initiation) can precipitate/worsen attacks and may obscure triggers
Recent dental procedure/trauma or stress as a trigger may lead to assumption of infection or medication reaction
Alimemazine sedation may mask early airway symptoms and complicate symptom reporting

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency/dysfunction (Type I or Type II)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary angioedema with normal C1-INH (HAE-nl-C1INH) (if complement studies are normal but clinical picture strongly consistent)
Acquired angioedema due to C1-INH deficiency (AAE) (if low C1q and later age of onset)
required_evidence: Recurrent angioedema episodes without urticaria/pruritus and/or recurrent abdominal pain episodes consistent with bowel wall edema
Low C4 complement level (especially during attacks; often persistently low)
Abnormal C1-INH testing: low antigenic level (Type I) and/or low functional activity (Type I/II)
Normal serum tryptase during episodes (argues against mast-cell mediated anaphylaxis)

expected_lab_results.C4 complement: Low (typically persistently low; may be markedly low during attacks)
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) antigenic: Low in Type I HAE; normal or elevated in Type II HAE
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor functional activit: Low (reduced) in Type I and Type II HAE
expected_lab_results.C1q complement: Normal in hereditary angioedema; low suggests acquired C1-INH deficiency
expected_lab_results.Serum tryptase: Normal (not elevated) unless concurrent anaphylaxis/mast-cell mediated process
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal; may show mild leukocytosis during stress/abdominal attacks but nonspecific

medications: Icatibant 30 mg subcutaneous once; may repeat every 6 hours as needed (maximum 3 doses in 24 hours) for acute HAE attack
C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (plasma-derived) 20 units/kg IV once for acute HAE attack (alternative first-line)
Fresh frozen plasma 2 units IV once if specific HAE therapies unavailable (use with caution; may worsen symptoms in some cases)
Ondansetron 4 mg IV/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting during abdominal attacks
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3 g/day typical adult)
non_pharmacological: Airway risk assessment each episode; urgent ED/ICU evaluation for tongue, floor-of-mouth, or laryngeal symptoms; early airway management if voice change/stridor/dyspnea
Avoid known triggers (trauma, dental procedures without prophylaxis, estrogen-containing contraceptives, ACE inhibitors); review medication list for bradykinin-mediated angioedema triggers
Provide an on-demand rescue plan and ensure access to at least 2 doses of on-demand therapy (e.g., icatibant or C1-INH) and training in self-administration
Consider short-term prophylaxis (e.g., C1-INH concentrate prior to high-risk procedures such as dental/surgical) in consultation with allergy/immunology
Referral to allergy/immunology for confirmation, family screening, and assessment for long-term prophylaxis (e.g., lanadelumab, berotralstat, or regular C1-INH) based on attack frequency/severity
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Allergy/Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after diagnosis or acute presentation; sooner (24-72 hours) after any airway-involved attack or ED visit

must_include: Condition is typically bradykinin-mediated angioedema (not histamine-mediated) so antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine often do not relieve HAE attacks
Explain inheritance/family implications and need for family member screening when appropriate
Discuss warning signs of airway involvement requiring emergency care
Acute attack options: icatibant or C1-INH concentrate as first-line; when and how to use on-demand therapy
Need for urgent evaluation for throat/tongue symptoms and possible airway management
Role of prophylaxis (short-term before procedures; long-term if frequent/severe attacks) and specialist follow-up
Confirm penicillin allergy history and reassure that first-line HAE therapies are not penicillins
Review current use of Alimemazine: may help sleep/itch but does not treat HAE pathophysiology; caution about sedation and not delaying emergency care
Shared decision-making on access to rescue meds, costs, and training for self-administration
Avoid ACE inhibitors and estrogen-containing therapies due to increased risk of attacks
Plan for procedures (dental/surgery) with short-term prophylaxis and carrying rescue medication
Provide written action plan and encourage medical alert identification

If diagnosis not already confirmed, order appropriate HAE workup to support/confirm diagnosis and subtype (e.g., C4, C1-INH antigenic level and function; consider C1q when acquired angioedema is possible).
Interpret results to distinguish HAE (low C4 with low/abnormal C1-INH) vs alternative causes; decide if further testing/referral is needed.
State working or confirmed diagnosis (hereditary angioedema) and document severity/urgency based on airway involvement.
Perform critical allergy check (including penicillin allergy details and any prior reactions to C1-INH products, icatibant, latex) before prescribing.
Review for drug interactions (non-critical) including sedating agents (e.g., alimemazine) and any planned therapies; ensure no interacting contraindicated meds are continued.

Perform critical contraindication check before prescribing; ensure no ACE inhibitor/estrogen exposure, assess pregnancy status when relevant, and confirm appropriateness of selected on-demand/prophylaxis therapy.

Prescribe evidence-based on-demand therapy (icatibant or plasma-derived/recombinant C1-INH) and/or prophylaxis when indicated; include dosing, route, quantity, refills, and self-administration supplies/training plan.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, bradykinin mechanism, inheritance/family screening, airway warning signs; explain treatment plan and emergency actions; address concerns (penicillin allergy, alimemazine limitations/sedation); lifestyle counseling (avoid ACEi/estrogen, procedure planning, action plan, medical alert).
Arrange specialist follow-up (allergy/immunology) and confirm access plan for rescue meds, training, and follow-up intervals based on attack frequency/severity.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and documentation of action plan.

Do not mention flushing, sudden loose stools, or heart racing unless specifically asked about episodes, diarrhea, or palpitations; then describe brief facial flushing with racing heart and occasional sudden diarrhea that resolves. If asked directly about accidents/incontinence from diarrhea, be embarrassed, minimize at first, then admit a few times you couldn’t reach the bathroom. comorbidities: Former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago)
confounders: Chlorthalidone-associated hyponatremia/hypokalemia can mimic or worsen endocrine findings
Omeprazole can elevate chromogranin A independent of neuroendocrine tumor activity
Intermittent albuterol use can contribute to tachycardia and tremor, complicating assessment of flushing/palpitations

Stress hyperglycemia and poorly controlled diabetes can overlap with Cushingoid metabolic abnormalities

correct_diagnosis.primary: Bronchial neuroendocrine tumor (pulmonary carcinoid tumor)
required_evidence: Endobronchial or lung mass on chest imaging (CT preferred) consistent with neuroendocrine tumor
Histopathologic confirmation from bronchoscopy/biopsy showing neuroendocrine features (chromogranin A and synaptophysin positivity) and grading (typical vs atypical)
Staging evaluation including assessment of nodal/distant disease (e.g., contrast CT chest/abdomen ± somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT when available)
Clinical correlation for symptoms of airway obstruction (cough, wheeze, recurrent pneumonia, hemoptysis) and/or carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhea) if present

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman who is anxious and speaks vaguely unless guided. Start with: you keep getting short of breath and tired, and your mom says you snore loudly and sometimes stop breathing at night. Early on, volunteer: you get winded climbing one flight of stairs, feel tired even after a full night, and you’ve had wide thumbs/big toes since childhood, thick eyebrows, and a “different” smile. If asked, add: morning headaches, dry mouth/sore throat, weight gain, nighttime heartburn/sour taste, past ear infections with ongoing hearing issues, needing extra help in school/planning, and constipation. Do not mention unless specifically asked: coughing/choking with thin liquids, heavy periods with exhaustion, easy bruising/paleness, small crowded jaw/teeth issues. Be resistant/embarrassed about bedwetting and teeth grinding; disclose only with direct, gentle questioning. If asked about mood, admit feeling down and occasional ‘what’s the point’ thoughts, no plan. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, give more detail, and ask about costs/insurance and fear a genetic diagnosis could hurt your job.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old woman who is anxious and speaks vaguely unless asked direct questions. Start with: “My mom says I keep gaining weight and I’m always tired, and she wants to know what’s wrong.” Volunteer: weight gain despite “not eating much,” daytime tiredness/need naps, getting out of breath on stairs, and that you’ve always learned slower and need help with many things. If asked about sleep: admit loud snoring, waking up choking, morning headaches, and falling asleep unintentionally (TV/car). If asked about eating/weight: say you don’t feel full and crave snacks at night; only if asked gently, admit secret overeating and shame about being weighed. If asked about periods, mood, bowels, skin: irregular/skip months, mood swings with plan changes, constipation, dark velvety skin on neck/armpits, rashes in skin folds. If asked about other issues: bedwetting sometimes, trouble hearing in noise/TV too loud, clumsiness/balance issues. Withhold skin-picking and bullying unless strong rapport. Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid; blame new allergy pill and energy drinks; mention mom thinks it’s “lazy habits.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, answer more, and ask about costs/insurance, healthy food affordability, and whether care could affect benefits/job.

diagnosis.primary: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome
required_evidence: History of developmental delay/intellectual disability with dysmorphic features
Early-onset obesity with hyperphagia/food-seeking behavior (or caregiver report of excessive appetite)
Short stature and/or small hands/feet; hypogonadism or pubertal delay (if age-appropriate)
Behavioral features (tantrums, rigidity, skin picking) and/or hypotonia history
Genetic confirmation recommended: abnormal 15q11-q13 methylation testing consistent with PWS (or paternal deletion/maternal UPD by further testing)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal to elevated (prediabetes/diabetes possible in obesity); often 5.7–6.4% if insulin resistance present
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Normal to mildly elevated (impaired fasting glucose possible)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to elevated (possible NAFLD in obesity)

medications: Semaglutide 0.25 mg subcutaneous weekly (titrate every 4 weeks as tolerated per obesity/diabetes indication and age-appropriateness)
Metformin 500 mg PO daily with food (titrate to 1000 mg PO BID as tolerated if insulin resistance/prediabetes/diabetes present)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with genetic testing (15q11-q13 methylation analysis; then deletion vs maternal UPD testing as needed) and refer to medical genetics
Structured food security plan (locked food access, supervised meals, calorie-controlled diet) and caregiver training; coordinate with school/group home if applicable
Multidisciplinary obesity management: dietitian, behavioral therapy, physical activity plan tailored to developmental level
Screen for and manage comorbidities: obstructive sleep apnea (sleep study if symptoms), hypertension, NAFLD, scoliosis, hypothyroidism, hypogonadism; consider endocrinology referral
Consider recombinant human growth hormone therapy if PWS confirmed and no contraindications (specialist-directed; requires evaluation for sleep apnea and glucose intolerance)
Developmental supports: speech/OT/PT as indicated; individualized education plan (IEP) and social services support
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4–6 weeks for weight/metabolic medication tolerance and lifestyle plan adherence; repeat metabolic labs in ~3 months

must_include: Working diagnosis is a genetic syndrome causing developmental delay and dysmorphic features with severe obesity risk (Prader-Willi syndrome)
Diagnosis requires genetic confirmation (15q11-q13 methylation testing) and explains hyperphagia/weight gain biology
Discuss common associated issues (sleep apnea, diabetes risk, fatty liver, hormonal/puberty issues, behavioral rigidity) and need for multidisciplinary care

Specialist-directed therapies may include growth hormone after appropriate screening

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected genetic obesity/developmental syndrome: developmental trajectory, hyperphagia/food-seeking, neonatal hypotonia/feeding issues, sleep/breathing symptoms, puberty/hormonal concerns, behavioral rigidity, family history, and current meds.
Order confirmatory genetic testing for Prader-Willi syndrome (15q11-q13 methylation analysis) and baseline comorbidity screening relevant to severe obesity and endocrine risk.
Review genetic test and baseline metabolic/endocrine results to support diagnosis and guide safe treatment choices.
Document working diagnosis (suspected/confirmed Prader-Willi syndrome) with rationale and differential considerations.
Explain diagnosis, need for genetic confirmation, expected associated issues, and multidisciplinary management plan; address caregiver concerns and provide safety planning for food access.
Perform required critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm amoxicillin urticarial rash and latex contact dermatitis and document avoidance/precautions.

Perform required critical safety check before prescribing obesity/insulin-resistance medications (e.g., metformin/GLP-1 RA) and any other agents; consider renal/hepatic disease, pancreatitis history, pregnancy, severe GI disease, etc.
Check for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions with current medications prior to prescribing (non-critical but expected).

If appropriate, initiate pharmacotherapy targeting obesity/insulin resistance (e.g., metformin; consider GLP-1 RA per age/indication) with monitoring plan; avoid contraindicated options and ensure allergies are respected.

Provide lifestyle/environmental management education: structured meals, portion control, restricted food access across settings, activity plan, and warning signs to report (hypoglycemia symptoms if on glucose-lowering meds, GI adverse effects, sleep apnea symptoms).

Arrange follow-up for results review, weight/metabolic monitoring, referrals (genetics, endocrinology, sleep medicine, nutrition, behavioral therapy), and consideration of growth hormone after appropriate screening.
Close encounter with clear return precautions and documented plan.
